subject: Physics
q: The drift velocity of electrons for a conductor connected in an electrical circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math>. The conductor is now replaced by another conductor with same material and same length but double the area of cross-section. The applied voltage remains same. The new drift velocity of electrons will be
question: The drift velocity of electrons for a conductor connected in an electrical circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math>. The conductor is now replaced by another conductor with same material and same length but double the area of cross-section. The applied voltage remains same. The new drift velocity of electrons will be
solution:
Drift velocity of electrons through a conductor kept in an external electric field is given by Vd=-eEmτ=-eVmlτ 

Where, e=magnitude of charge on electron, m=mass of electron, E=external electric field and τ=average relaxation time.

As, drift velocity is independent of area. So, the new drift velocity of electrons will be Vd.

subject: Physics
q: A battery with EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$ is connected across a resistance $R$. The power consumption in $R$ will be maximum when :
question: A battery with EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$ is connected across a resistance $R$. The power consumption in $R$ will be maximum when :
solution:
Power consumed in external resistance R is $P = \frac{E^2 R}{(R + r)^2}$.
To find maximum, differentiate with respect to R:
$\frac{dP}{dR} = E^2 \frac{(R + r)^2 - 2R(R + r)}{(R + r)^4} = E^2 \frac{r - R}{(R + r)^3}$.
$\frac{dP}{dR} = 0$ gives $R = r$.
This is the maximum power transfer theorem: maximum power is delivered when the external load resistance equals the source internal resistance.

subject: Physics
q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> potential difference is applied across a wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the drift speed of electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the electron density in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the resistivity of the material is close to:
question: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> potential difference is applied across a wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the drift speed of electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the electron density in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the resistivity of the material is close to:
solution:
Potential difference= 5 V, length =0.1 m=l and the electron speed = drift velocity vd=2.5×10-4 m s-1, electron density n = 8 × 1 0 2 8 m - 3  and the charge on each electron(e) =1.6×10-19 C. We know i=nAevd  ...(i). And V=iR        ...(ii)



Resistance R is also equal to, ρlA⇒R=ρlA

ρ=ARl        ρ = Resistivity

=Al×Vi     [from (ii)]=Avl×nA evd & [from (i)]=Vl×n×e×vd

= 5 0.1 × 8 × 1 0 2 8 × 1.6 × 1 0 - 1 9 × 2.5 × 1 0 - 4

=0.156×10-4 Ω m=1.56×10-5 Ω m

subject: Physics
q: In the circuit shown, the potential difference between $\mathrm{A}$ and $B$ is
question: In the circuit shown, the potential difference between $\mathrm{A}$ and $B$ is
solution:
Given, $E_{1}=1 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{E}_{2}=2 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{E}_{3}=3 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{r}_{1}=1 \Omega$

$\mathrm{r}_{2}=1 \Omega$ and $\mathrm{r}_{3}=1 \Omega$

$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{CD}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}_{1}}+\frac{\mathrm{E}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{E}_{3}}{\mathrm{r}_{3}}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_{3}}}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1}+\frac{3}{1}}=\frac{6}{3}=2 \mathrm{~V}$

subject: Physics
q:
The variation of applied potential and current flowing through a given wire is shown in figure. The length of wire is 31.4 cm. The diameter of wire is measured as 2.4 cm. The resistivity of the given wire is measured as x×10-3 Ω cm. The value of x is _____ .

[Take π=3.14]
question:
The variation of applied potential and current flowing through a given wire is shown in figure. The length of wire is 31.4 cm. The diameter of wire is measured as 2.4 cm. The resistivity of the given wire is measured as x×10-3 Ω cm. The value of x is _____ .

[Take π=3.14]
solution:
Since, slope of V−I graph represents the resistance, so R=tan45°=1 Ω

Now, resistance of wire is given by R=ρLA 

So, resistivity of the wire is ρ=ARL=πr2RL=π1.22×10-431.4×10-2=1.44×10-3 Ω m=144×10-3Ω cm

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> connected across the right gap of a meter bridge. When a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected on the left gap by using a resistance box, the balance length is found to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the left end. If the resistivity of the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is :
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> connected across the right gap of a meter bridge. When a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected on the left gap by using a resistance box, the balance length is found to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the left end. If the resistivity of the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is :
solution:
From the null point condition of a Meter Bridge, it follows that,

4.5 Ω60=X100-60⇒X=4060×4.5 Ω=3 Ω

Also,

X=ρlA=ρlπr2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

3=ρ×0.1π×7×10-42⇒ρ=3×π×7×10-420.1≈66×10-7 Ω m

Therefore, R=66.

q: First, a set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> equal resistors of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> each are connected in series to a battery of E.M.F. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> A current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is observed to flow. It is observed that the current is increased <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> times, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is ___________.
question: First, a set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> equal resistors of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> each are connected in series to a battery of E.M.F. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> A current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is observed to flow. Then, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> resistors are connected in parallel to the same battery. It is observed that the current is increased <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> times, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is ___________.
solution:
When the resistances of each 10 Ω â€‹ are connected in series and parallel then,Rs=10n, and Rp=10n. Given, ip=20is, 

⇒2010n+10=202010n+10⇒20n10+10n=202010n+10

⇒n=20.

q: If you are provided a set of resistances, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Connect these resistances to obtain an equivalent resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>46</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
question: If you are provided a set of resistances, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Connect these resistances to obtain an equivalent resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>46</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are in parallel with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> in series

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are in parallel with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> in series

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are in parallel with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> in series

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are in parallel with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> in series
solution:
We can arrange the circuit like above now on solving we get a simplified circuit, 

here, both the resistance are connected in series then resultant resistance will be, 

Req=43 Ω+14 Ω=463 Ω, in the other case we can not get this result.

subject: Physics
q: In meter bridge experiment for measuring unknown resistance 'S', the null point is obtained at a distance 30 cm from the left side as shown at point D. If R is 5.6 kΩ, then the value of unknown resistance 'S' will be _____ Ω.
question: In meter bridge experiment for measuring unknown resistance 'S', the null point is obtained at a distance 30 cm from the left side as shown at point D. If R is 5.6 kΩ, then the value of unknown resistance 'S' will be _____ Ω.
solution:
Using balanced wheatstone condition in meter bridge

RS=7030

Given here, R=5.6 kΩ.

The, S30=5.6×10370

S=37×5.6×103=2400 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across series combination of two resistors each of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. A voltmeter of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is used to measure the potential difference across each resistor. The reading of the voltmeter will be:
question: A cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across series combination of two resistors each of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. A voltmeter of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is used to measure the potential difference across each resistor. The reading of the voltmeter will be:
solution:
As we know, voltmeter is connected in parallel to the element being measured.

The equivalent resistance of the above circuit is Req=400×100400+100+100=80+100=180 Ω

Current through the circuit is i=90180=12 A

Reading of the voltmeter is VR=i400×100400+100=12×80=40 V

subject: Physics
q: The resistance per centimeter of a meter bridge wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance in left gap. Balancing length from left end is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance in right gap. Now the wire is replaced by another wire of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math> resistance per centimeter. The new balancing length for same settings will be at
question: The resistance per centimeter of a meter bridge wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance in left gap. Balancing length from left end is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance in right gap. Now the wire is replaced by another wire of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math> resistance per centimeter. The new balancing length for same settings will be at
solution:
For first case:

Xrl1=25rl2 ... (i)

For second case:

X2rl'1=252rl'2 ... (ii)

From (i) and (ii), we get

l'1l1=l'2l2 also total length is constant.

Therefore, balance length doesn't change and 

l'1=l1=40 cm

q: In the given figure, the value of V0 will be _____ V.
question: In the given figure, the value of V0 will be _____ V.
solution:
Using Kirchhoff's junction rule,

V0-21 kΩ+V0-41 kΩ+V0-61 kΩ=0

3V0-12=0

V0=4 V

subject: Physics
q: A wire of resistance $R$ is bent into a triangular pyramid as shown in figure with each segment having same length. The resistance between points $A$ and $B$ is $R / n$. The value of $n$ is :
question: A wire of resistance $R$ is bent into a triangular pyramid as shown in figure with each segment having same length. The resistance between points $A$ and $B$ is $R / n$. The value of $n$ is :
solution:
As $r=\frac{R}{6}$

(As balanced wheat stone bridge is formed) Now, Equivalent resistance between A and B can be written as
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{AB}}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{r}}+\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{r}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{12}$

subject: Physics
q: A cell of internal resistance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> drives current through an external resistance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power delivered by the cell to the external resistance will be maximum when:
question: A cell of internal resistance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> drives current through an external resistance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power delivered by the cell to the external resistance will be maximum when:
solution:
Current i=Er+R
Power generated in R

P=i2R
P=E2R(r+R)2

for maximum power dPdR=0
E2(r+R)2×1-R×2(r+R)(r+R)4=0
⇒when, r=R then, dPdR=0

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of the equivalent resistance of the network (shown in figure) between the points a and b when switch is open and switch is closed is x: 8. The value of x is
question: The ratio of the equivalent resistance of the network (shown in figure) between the points a and b when switch is open and switch is closed is x: 8. The value of x is
solution:
When switch is closed

R1=2R3+2R3=4R3

When open R2=3R·3R3R+3R=3R2

R1R2=4R3×23R=89=8x

subject: Physics
q: In a conductor, if the number of conduction electrons per unit volume is 8.5×1028  m-3 and mean free time is 25 fs (femto second), it’s approximate resistivity is: me=9.1×10-31 kg
question: In a conductor, if the number of conduction electrons per unit volume is 8.5×1028  m-3 and mean free time is 25 fs (femto second), it’s approximate resistivity is: me=9.1×10-31 kg
solution:
We know i=neAvd
⇒i=VR=VAρl=EAρ
ρ=EAi=EAneAvd
Drift velocity can be written as vd=eEmeτ
ρ=EAmeneAeEτ=mene2τ
=9.1×10-318.5×1028×1.6×10-192×25×10-15
≈1.65×10-8 Ω m

q: An energy source will supply a constant current into the load of its internal resistance is
question: An energy source will supply a constant current into the load of its internal resistance is
options: equal to the resistance of the load.
very large as compared to the load resistance.
non-zero but less than the resistance of the load.
solution: $I=\frac{E_0}{R+r} \square \frac{E}{r}$ if $R < r$

q: Four bulbs B1, B2, B3 and B4 of 100 W each are connected to 220 V main as shown in the figure. The reading in an ideal ammeter will be
question: Four bulbs B1, B2, B3 and B4 of 100 W each are connected to 220 V main as shown in the figure. The reading in an ideal ammeter will be
solution:
The resistance of any bulb=V2/P

Current through ammeter at shown position=3i1

current in any 1 bulb=i1=VR=VV2/P=PV=100220

Through ammeter, ⇒inet=3i1=300220=1.35 A

A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply having an internal resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across one of its sides. The potential drop across the diagonals of the square loop is __________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> wire is bent to form a square loop. A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply having an internal resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across one of its sides. The potential drop across the diagonals of the square loop is __________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Here assume current as

By KVL in the outer loop

9-12i-4i=0

16i=9

8i=92=4.5

=45×10-1

subject: Physics
q: A galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ when connected in series with $400 \Omega$ measures a voltage of upto $10 \mathrm{~V}$. The value of resistance required to convert the galvanometer into ammeter to read upto $10 \mathrm{~A}$ is $x \times 10^{-2} \Omega$. The value of $x$ is :
question: A galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ when connected in series with $400 \Omega$ measures a voltage of upto $10 \mathrm{~V}$. The value of resistance required to convert the galvanometer into ammeter to read upto $10 \mathrm{~A}$ is $x \times 10^{-2} \Omega$. The value of $x$ is :
solution:
$\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{10}{400+100}=20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$
For ammeter
Let shunt resistance $=\mathrm{S}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{R}=\left(\mathrm{i}-\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}\right) \mathrm{S} \\
& 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 100=10 \mathrm{~S} \\
& \mathrm{~S}=20 \times 10^{-2} \Omega
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In an electric circuit, a call of certain emf provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> However, it provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>The emf of the cell is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
question: In an electric circuit, a call of certain emf provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> However, it provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>The emf of the cell is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Terminal voltage, v=iR=ERR+r

1st→1.25=E(5)5+r  …i
2nd→1=E(2)2+r  …ii

By i and ii,
r=1 Ω, E=32 V=1510 volt
⇒x=15

subject: Physics
q: Two cells of emf 1 V and 2 V and internal resistance $2 \Omega$ and $1 \Omega$, respectively, are connected in series with an external resistance of $6 \Omega$. The total current in the circuit is $\mathrm{I}_1$. Now the same two cells in parallel configuration are connected to same external resistance. In this case, the total current drawn is $I_2$. The value of $\left(\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right)$ is $\frac{x}{3}$. The value of $x$ is _____.
question: Two cells of emf 1 V and 2 V and internal resistance $2 \Omega$ and $1 \Omega$, respectively, are connected in series with an external resistance of $6 \Omega$. The total current in the circuit is $\mathrm{I}_1$. Now the same two cells in parallel configuration are connected to same external resistance. In this case, the total current drawn is $I_2$. The value of $\left(\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right)$ is $\frac{x}{3}$. The value of $x$ is _____.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=3 \\ & \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=9 \\ & \mathrm{i}_1=\frac{3}{9}=\frac{1}{3}\end{aligned}$
$\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{\mathrm{r}_2}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}}$
$\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{2}{1}}{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{1}}=\frac{5}{3}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}_{\text {equ }}=\frac{2 \times 1}{3}+6=\frac{20}{3} \\ & \mathrm{i}_2=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{i}_1}{\mathrm{i}_2}=\frac{4}{3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A potentiometer wire of length 300 cm is connected in series with a resistance 780 Ω and a standard cell of emf 4 V. A constant current flows through potentiometer wire. The length of the null point for cell of emf 20mV is found to be 60 cm. The resistance of the potentiometer wire is _____ Ω.
question: A potentiometer wire of length 300 cm is connected in series with a resistance 780 Ω and a standard cell of emf 4 V. A constant current flows through potentiometer wire. The length of the null point for cell of emf 20mV is found to be 60 cm. The resistance of the potentiometer wire is _____ Ω.
solution:
Let resistance of potentiometers wire is R, then the current through AB

iAB=4R+780

Potential difference across AB

VAB=4RR+780

Potential difference across AC

VAC=VABlL=4RR+780×60300=4R5R+780

This should be equal to 20 mV

⇒4R5R+780=20×10-3=2×10-2

⇒4R=10-1R+780

⇒4R-R10=78

⇒39R10=78

⇒R=20 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A meter bridge with two resistances $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ as shown in figure was balanced (null point) at 40 cm from the point $P$. The null point changed to 50 cm from the point $P$, when $16 \Omega$ resistance is connected in parallel to $R_{2}$. The values of resistances $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ are $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A meter bridge with two resistances $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ as shown in figure was balanced (null point) at 40 cm from the point $P$. The null point changed to 50 cm from the point $P$, when $16 \Omega$ resistance is connected in parallel to $R_{2}$. The values of resistances $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ are $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Initial balance at 40 cm: $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{40}{60} = \frac{2}{3}$ ...(i)
With $16\,\Omega$ parallel to $R_2$, null point at 50 cm:
$\frac{R_1}{\frac{16R_2}{16+R_2}} = \frac{50}{50} = 1 \Rightarrow R_1 = \frac{16R_2}{16+R_2}$ ...(ii)
From (i): $R_1 = \frac{2}{3}R_2$.
Substituting in (ii): $\frac{2}{3}R_2 = \frac{16R_2}{16+R_2}$.
$\frac{2}{3} = \frac{16}{16+R_2} \Rightarrow 2(16+R_2) = 48 \Rightarrow R_2 = 8\,\Omega$.
$R_1 = \frac{16}{3}\,\Omega$.

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length 25 m and cross-sectional area $5 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ having resistivity of $2 \times 10^{-6} \Omega \mathrm{~m}$ is bent into a complete circle. The resistance between diametrically opposite points will be
question: A wire of length 25 m and cross-sectional area $5 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ having resistivity of $2 \times 10^{-6} \Omega \mathrm{~m}$ is bent into a complete circle. The resistance between diametrically opposite points will be
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{L}=25 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{~A}=5 \mathrm{~mm}^2=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2 \\ & \rho=2 \times 10^{-6} \Omega \mathrm{~m} \\ & \mathrm{R}_{\text {wire }}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 25}{5 \times 10^{-6}}=10 \\ & \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}=\frac{10}{4}=2.5 \Omega
\end{aligned}$
Answer does not match with NTA option.

question: A heater coil is cut into two equal parts and only one part is now used in the heater. The heat generated will now be

question: In the given circuit, the current I through the battery will be

subject: Physics
q: In a meter bridge experiment to determine the value of unknown resistance, first the resistances $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the left and right gaps of the bridge and the null point is obtained at a distance $l \mathrm{~cm}$ from the left. Now when an unknown resistance $x \Omega$ is connected in parallel to $3 \Omega$ resistance, the null point is shifted by 10 cm to the right of wire. The value of unknown resistance $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: In a meter bridge experiment to determine the value of unknown resistance, first the resistances $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the left and right gaps of the bridge and the null point is obtained at a distance $l \mathrm{~cm}$ from the left. Now when an unknown resistance $x \Omega$ is connected in parallel to $3 \Omega$ resistance, the null point is shifted by 10 cm to the right of wire. The value of unknown resistance $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
Meter bridge null point condition: $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{l}{100-l}$
Initial: $R_1 = 2Ω$, $R_2 = 3Ω$, null at $l$ cm
$\frac{2}{3} = \frac{l}{100-l}$ gives $2(100-l) = 3l$, so $l = 40$ cm
When $xΩ$ connected in parallel with $3Ω$: $R_2' = \frac{3x}{3+x}Ω$
New null point at $(40+10) = 50$ cm from left:
$\frac{2}{R_2'} = \frac{50}{50} = 1$
$\frac{2(3+x)}{3x} = 1$ gives $6 + 2x = 3x$
$x = 6Ω$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is cut into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> equal parts. If these parts are joined parallely, then resultant resistance will be :
question: A wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is cut into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> equal parts. If these parts are joined parallely, then resultant resistance will be :
solution:
Resistance of each part will be equally divided. So, the resistance of each part of the wire can be expressed as R5.

The formula to calculate the equivalent resistance of the combination of resistors is given by

1Req=∑i=151Ri   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

1Req=5R+5R+5R+5R+5R=25R⇒Req=R25

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The equivalent resistance of resistors in a series combination is smaller than least resistance used in the combination.

Statement II : The resistivity of the material is independent of temperature. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The equivalent resistance of resistors in a series combination is smaller than least resistance used in the combination.

Statement II : The resistivity of the material is independent of temperature. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
solution:
The formula for series equivalent resistance is given by 

Rs=R1+R2+......+Rn. 

Hence, the equivalent resistance will be the highest value compared to other resistances.

The resistivity of a material is given by,

ρ=ρ01+αΔT

Clearly, the resistivity is dependant on temperature.

Hence, this is the right option.

q: A galvanometer has a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> and it allows maximum current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. It can be converted into voltmeter to measure upto <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by connecting in series a resistor of resistance.
question: A galvanometer has a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> and it allows maximum current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. It can be converted into voltmeter to measure upto <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by connecting in series a resistor of resistance.
solution:
If we connect a high resistance(R) in series with the galvanometer, it can be successfully converted into a voltmeter. Therefore,

Voltage drop across voltmeter:

V=IgRg+R⇒100=Ig50+R

⇒R=1005×10-3-50

=19950 Ω

q: A wire of resistance $9 \Omega$ is bent to form an equilateral triangle. Then the equivalent resistance across any two vertices will be _____ ohm.
question: A wire of resistance $9 \Omega$ is bent to form an equilateral triangle. Then the equivalent resistance across any two vertices will be _____ ohm.
solution: $9 \Omega$ is the resistance of whole wire $\therefore$ resistance of each wire $=3 \Omega$. $\therefore$ Equivalent resistance $=2 \Omega$

subject: Physics
q: A material ' $B$ ' has twice the specific resistance of ' $A$ '. A circular wire made of ' $B$ ' has twice the diameter of a wire made of ' $A$ '. Then for the two wires to have the same resistance, the ratio $\ell_A / \ell_B$ of their respective lengths must be
question: A material ' $B$ ' has twice the specific resistance of ' $A$ '. A circular wire made of ' $B$ ' has twice the diameter of a wire made of ' $A$ '. Then for the two wires to have the same resistance, the ratio $\ell_A / \ell_B$ of their respective lengths must be
solution:
$\mathrm{R}_1=\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{A}} \ell_{\mathrm{A}}}{\pi \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}^2} \quad \mathrm{R}_2=\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{B}} \ell_{\mathrm{B}}}{\pi \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}^2}$
$\frac{\ell_{\mathrm{A}}}{\ell_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}^2}{\rho_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}^2}=\frac{2 \rho_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}^2}{\rho_{\mathrm{A}} \cdot 4 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}^2} \Rightarrow \frac{\ell_{\mathrm{B}}}{\ell_{\mathrm{A}}}=2$
